Quick Paths
2505.12716 • 2025

Shadow-FT: Tuning Instruct Model via Training on Paired Base Model

A simple, cost-free method that fine-tunes the BASE model and transplants weight updates to INSTRUCT, consistently outperforming conventional fine-tuning across 8+ model families.

Authors: Taiqiang Wu, Runming Yang, Jiayi Li, Pengfei Hu, Yik-Chung Wu, Ngai Wong, Yujiu Yang
Institutions: Tsinghua University, The University of Hong Kong
§ 1

TL;DR

Key Insight
Shadow-FT fine-tunes the BASE model instead of INSTRUCT, then grafts weight updates onto INSTRUCT, consistently outperforming conventional tuning by 1–4 average points across 8+ model families and 19 benchmarks with zero additional cost.
<3%
Weight gap (σ)
+3.7
Avg improvement (Qwen-3-4B)
8+
Model families tested
19
Benchmarks covered
Bottom Line
If you are fine-tuning an INSTRUCT model and seeing marginal gains or degradation, try Shadow-FT: fine-tune the BASE version instead and transplant the weight delta. It is free, simple, and consistently better.
§ 2

The Problem: INSTRUCT Degeneration

Key Insight
Directly fine-tuning INSTRUCT models leads to marginal improvements and often performance degeneration — for example, Qwen-3-4B loses 2.3 average points with conventional LoRA tuning compared to the untuned INSTRUCT baseline.

When practitioners fine-tune an INSTRUCT model on task-specific data, they expect improvements. But the opposite frequently happens: performance drops across math, code, and reasoning benchmarks. The core reason is that the instruction-following prior embedded during post-training creates a rigid optimization landscape.

The INSTRUCT model starts training with abnormally high gradients (reflecting a mismatch between its instruction-tuned priors and the new training objective) but these gradients collapse rapidly, meaning the model quickly enters a regime where it resists further learning.

Analogy

Think of it like teaching a new dance routine to two different students. The first student (BASE) is a blank slate with no prior dance training — they pick up new moves smoothly and steadily. The second student (INSTRUCT) is a classically trained ballet dancer — their years of disciplined training make them a beautiful performer, but when you try to teach them hip-hop, their ingrained muscle memory constantly fights the new movements. They start with intense effort (high initial gradients) but quickly fall back into their old patterns (gradient collapse). Shadow-FT solves this by teaching the new routine to the blank-slate student and then transplanting just those new moves onto the ballet dancer.

Training Dynamics: Evidence of Degeneration

Figure 5: Training dynamics — loss curves (left) and gradient norms (right) for BASE vs INSTRUCT fine-tuning.
What This Shows

The left panel shows training loss over steps. INSTRUCT loss starts 22.6% higher than BASE, indicating a worse initial fit to the training data due to its instruction-following prior conflicting with the SFT objective.

The right panel shows gradient norms. INSTRUCT gradients start 3.25x larger than BASE — evidence of the optimization conflict. Critically, INSTRUCT gradients then decay to just 1/61 of their initial value, meaning the model quickly stops learning. BASE shows smooth, steady optimization throughout.

How to Read This
Start here: Compare the two curves in the right panel. The orange (INSTRUCT/FT) line spikes high then crashes — this is the gradient collapse that causes degeneration.
Then look at: The left panel loss curves. Despite higher initial gradients, INSTRUCT does not converge to a better loss, confirming that the high gradients were not productive.
Key Takeaway

INSTRUCT models are poor learners: their post-training prior creates an optimization mismatch that manifests as high initial gradients followed by rapid gradient collapse. BASE models learn smoothly.

Specific Degeneration Numbers

The degeneration effect is starkly visible in the Qwen-3-4B results. Conventional LoRA fine-tuning on the INSTRUCT model produces these changes versus the untuned INSTRUCT baseline:

Benchmark Group INSTRUCT (baseline) Conventional FT Change
Math-7 73.8 71.2 -2.6
Code-3 66.4 59.6 -6.8
Knowledge-9 63.7 61.1 -2.6
Diagnosing Degeneration in Practice ▼

Signs that your INSTRUCT fine-tuning is suffering from degeneration:

  • Training loss spikes then plateaus early — compare with a BASE run if possible.
  • Gradient norms drop rapidly in the first 10–20% of training — this indicates the model is reverting to its prior.
  • Downstream benchmarks regress on capabilities not directly related to your training data (e.g., math performance drops when training on code data).
  • The model becomes less conversational — instruction-following abilities degrade, producing raw or incomplete outputs.

If you observe these symptoms, Shadow-FT is a strong candidate for improvement.

§ 3

Key Observation: Weight Similarity

Key Insight
BASE and INSTRUCT model weights differ by less than 3% on average (σ < 0.03), meaning post-training barely changes the actual weight values despite dramatically changing model behavior.

Despite massive behavioral differences between BASE and INSTRUCT — one generates raw continuations while the other follows instructions, formats responses, and refuses harmful queries — the underlying weight matrices are nearly identical.

The sigma (σ) metric measures element-wise absolute difference normalized by combined magnitudes. For Llama-3.1-8B, sigma is just 0.018. This is surprising because post-training involves multiple stages (SFT, RLHF/DPO, etc.) yet only nudges weights by tiny amounts.

Analogy

Two editions of the same 1000-page textbook. The first edition is raw reference material (BASE), the second has study guides, practice questions, and highlighted concepts (INSTRUCT). Page by page, 97% of text is identical — the editors improved the book dramatically by changing only a small fraction.

The Sigma Metric

\[ \sigma(W_B, W_I) = \frac{\| W_B - W_I \|_1}{\| W_B \|_1 + \| W_I \|_1} \]
Equation 1: Weight Similarity Metric

Where \(W_B\) and \(W_I\) are the BASE and INSTRUCT weight matrices respectively. The numerator computes the L1 (element-wise absolute) distance between the two weight sets, while the denominator normalizes by their combined L1 magnitudes. A value of 0 means identical weights; a value approaching 1 means completely different weights.

Figure 2: 3D weight distributions for Llama-3.1-8B showing BASE (left) and INSTRUCT (middle) weights are nearly identical, with the gap (right) zoomed 10x.
What This Shows

Each subfigure shows the weight distribution as a 3D surface for layer.0.k_proj. The left (BASE) and middle (INSTRUCT) surfaces are visually indistinguishable. The right panel shows their difference, zoomed in 10x, revealing the gap is negligible with an average sigma value of 0.016.

How to Read This
Start here: Compare the left (Base Model) and middle (Instruct Model) panels. They look virtually identical.
Key observation: The right panel (Gap) is zoomed 10x on the z-axis. Even with this magnification, the differences are tiny (scale of 0.01), confirming the <3% sigma difference.
Key Takeaway

Post-training (SFT + RLHF) changes model behavior dramatically but the weight changes are minuscule — this is what makes weight delta transplantation feasible.

Sigma Values Across Model Families

Model Sigma (σ) Interpretation
Llama-3.1-8B 0.018 1.8% difference
Qwen-2.5-32B 0.004 0.4% difference (most similar)
Qwen-3-14B 0.027 2.7% difference

Computing Sigma: Code

Python — Computing Sigma with PyTorch + HuggingFace 
import torch
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM

# Load both model variants
base_model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    "meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B",
    torch_dtype=torch.float16,
    device_map="auto"
)
inst_model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    "meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct",
    torch_dtype=torch.float16,
    device_map="auto"
)

# Compute sigma
numerator = 0.0
denominator = 0.0
for (name_b, param_b), (name_i, param_i) in zip(
    base_model.named_parameters(),
    inst_model.named_parameters()
):
    assert name_b == name_i, f"Mismatch: {name_b} vs {name_i}"
    numerator += torch.sum(torch.abs(param_b - param_i)).item()
    denominator += (
        torch.sum(torch.abs(param_b)).item()
        + torch.sum(torch.abs(param_i)).item()
    )

sigma = numerator / denominator
print(f"Sigma: {sigma:.4f}")
# Expected output for Llama-3.1-8B: ~0.018
Practical Note

Computing sigma requires loading both models simultaneously. For large models (70B+), use torch_dtype=torch.float16 or bfloat16 and device_map="auto" across multiple GPUs. You can also compute sigma layer-by-layer to reduce memory.

§ 4

Methodology: Shadow-FT Framework

Key Insight
Shadow-FT fine-tunes the BASE model instead of INSTRUCT, then grafts the learned weight updates onto the INSTRUCT model, consistently outperforming conventional tuning with zero additional training cost.

Three-Step Framework

Shadow-FT operates in three simple steps:

  • Step 1 — Take the BASE model: Start with the pre-trained base model (e.g., Llama-3.1-8B), not the instruct variant.
  • Step 2 — Fine-tune normally: Apply standard supervised fine-tuning (full-parameter or LoRA) on your task-specific data. The BASE model, free of instruction-following priors, learns smoothly.
  • Step 3 — Compute and transplant the weight delta: Calculate \(\Delta W = W_B^{\text{tuned}} - W_B\) (the learned update), then add it to the INSTRUCT model: \(W_I^+ = W_I + \Delta W\).

This works because the BASE model is a better "learner" — without the instruction-following prior interfering, it optimizes smoothly and produces high-quality weight updates. Since BASE and INSTRUCT weights are nearly identical (σ < 0.03), these updates transfer seamlessly.

Analogy

Using a blank notebook (BASE) to take class notes, then copying those notes into an already well-organized notebook (INSTRUCT). Writing directly in the organized notebook would mess up existing annotations and formatting.

Figure 1: Motivation and framework overview of Shadow-FT.
What This Shows

This figure contains three subfigures that together tell the story of Shadow-FT:

(a) Vanilla SFT on Base/Instruct — BASE is a good learner but weak at tasks; INSTRUCT has marginal improvement and even degeneration from SFT.

(b) Weight similarity — BASE and INSTRUCT models have highly similar weights (σ < 0.03) across Llama-3.1-8B (0.018), Qwen-2.5-32B (0.004), and Qwen-3-14B (0.027).

(c) The Shadow-FT framework — Learn on Base, compute weight delta \(\Delta_B = W_B^+ - W_B\), add to Instruct to get better performance with the same training costs.

How to Read This
Start here: Look at subfigure (c) to understand the workflow: train BASE, compute delta, apply to INSTRUCT.
Then check: (a) shows why direct INSTRUCT tuning fails (progress bars show degeneration), and (b) shows why delta transfer works (weights are nearly identical).
Key Takeaway

Shadow-FT is elegant in its simplicity: just train on BASE instead of INSTRUCT. The near-identical weights ensure the delta transfers cleanly, and the superior optimization landscape of BASE ensures the delta is higher quality.

Mathematical Formulation

\[ W_I^+ = W_I + \underbrace{(\text{Tune}(W_B) - W_B)}_{\text{weight delta from BASE}} \]
Equation 2: Shadow-FT (Full-Parameter)

Where \(W_I\) is the INSTRUCT model, \(W_B\) is the BASE model, and \(\text{Tune}(W_B)\) is the BASE model after fine-tuning. The weight delta learned from BASE is directly added to the INSTRUCT weights.

Compare this with conventional fine-tuning:

\[ W_I^+ = W_I + \underbrace{(\text{Tune}(W_I) - W_I)}_{\text{weight delta from INSTRUCT}} \]
Equation 3: Conventional Fine-Tuning

In conventional FT, the delta is learned from the INSTRUCT model itself. As shown in Section 2, this delta is lower quality due to the optimization mismatch.

For LoRA, the formulation is even simpler:

\[ W_I^+ = W_I + \underbrace{B \cdot A}_{\text{LoRA adapter trained on BASE}} \]
Equation 4: Shadow-FT with LoRA

Train the LoRA adapter (matrices \(B\) and \(A\)) on the BASE model, then simply load the same adapter onto the INSTRUCT model. No additional computation needed beyond swapping which model the adapter is loaded onto.

Why BASE Deltas Transfer

Since \(\sigma(W_B, W_I) < 0.03\), the weight spaces are nearly identical. A delta that improves performance starting from \(W_B\) will have a very similar effect when applied starting from \(W_I\), because the local loss landscape geometry is essentially the same.

Implementation Pseudocode

Python — Shadow-FT with LoRA (Recommended) 
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer
from peft import get_peft_model, LoraConfig
from trl import SFTTrainer, SFTConfig

# Step 1: Load BASE model
base_model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    "meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B",  # BASE, not Instruct!
    torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    device_map="auto"
)
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B")

# Step 2: Configure and train LoRA on BASE
lora_config = LoraConfig(
    r=64,              # rank
    lora_alpha=128,    # scaling factor
    target_modules=["q_proj", "k_proj", "v_proj", "o_proj",
                     "gate_proj", "up_proj", "down_proj"],
    lora_dropout=0.05,
    task_type="CAUSAL_LM"
)
base_model = get_peft_model(base_model, lora_config)

trainer = SFTTrainer(
    model=base_model,
    train_dataset=your_dataset,
    args=SFTConfig(
        num_train_epochs=3,
        learning_rate=1e-4,
        per_device_train_batch_size=4,
        gradient_accumulation_steps=8,
        bf16=True,
        output_dir="./shadow_ft_lora"
    ),
)
trainer.train()
trainer.save_model("./shadow_ft_lora/adapter")

# Step 3: Load adapter onto INSTRUCT model
instruct_model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    "meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct",  # Now load INSTRUCT
    torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    device_map="auto"
)
# Simply load the BASE-trained adapter onto INSTRUCT
from peft import PeftModel
shadow_model = PeftModel.from_pretrained(
    instruct_model,
    "./shadow_ft_lora/adapter"
)
# Done! shadow_model is the Shadow-FT result.

Hyperparameters

Parameter Full-Param FT LoRA
Learning Rate 1e-5 1e-4
Epochs 3 3
Batch Size (global) 128 128
Max Sequence Length 2048 2048
LoRA Rank (r) — 64
LoRA Alpha — 128
Warmup Ratio 0.1 0.1
LR Scheduler cosine cosine
Practical Tips ▼
  • LoRA is recommended over full-parameter for most use cases. It is cheaper and often matches or exceeds full-param performance on Shadow-FT.
  • Use the same chat template: Train the BASE model with the INSTRUCT model's chat template applied to the training data. This ensures the adapter learns to produce outputs compatible with the INSTRUCT model's expected format.
  • No scaling factor needed: Unlike Chat Vector methods, Shadow-FT applies the delta with a coefficient of 1.0 — no hyperparameter search required.
  • Infrastructure: The paper used 8x A100 80GB GPUs with LLaMA-Factory for training and OpenCompass for evaluation.
§ 5

Results

Key Insight
Shadow-FT outperforms both vanilla INSTRUCT and conventional fine-tuning across 8+ model families and 19 benchmarks, with best improvements of +3.7 on Qwen-3-4B and +2.5 on Qwen-3-14B.

The authors conducted extensive validation covering Qwen-3 (4B, 8B, 14B), Qwen-2.5 (3B, 7B, 14B, 32B), Llama-3.2 (1B, 3B), and Llama-3.1 (8B) families. Both full-parameter and LoRA training were tested. The results are striking in their consistency: Shadow-FT wins nearly every combination of model family, size, and training method.

Table 1: Main results — average performance across 19 benchmarks for multiple model families and training methods.
What This Shows

This table compares three conditions for each model: (1) untuned INSTRUCT baseline, (2) conventional FT (Full and LoRA on INSTRUCT), and (3) Shadow-FT (Full and LoRA on BASE, applied to INSTRUCT). The "Avg." column shows the average across all 19 benchmarks.

How to Read This
Start here: Look at the "Avg." column for each model. Compare "Shadow-FT" rows (green highlighted) vs "FT" rows.
Key pattern: Shadow-FT rows consistently show higher averages, often by 1–4 points. Conventional FT sometimes degrades below the vanilla INSTRUCT baseline.
Key Takeaway

Shadow-FT is not a marginal improvement — it is consistently and substantially better than conventional fine-tuning, and it turns models that would have been degraded by conventional FT into models that genuinely improve.

Standout Results

Qwen-3-4B: Shadow-FT achieves 69.4 avg (+1.4 over INSTRUCT), while conventional FT drops to 65.7 (-2.3). That is a 3.7 point swing in favor of Shadow-FT.

Qwen-3-14B: Shadow-FT achieves 75.9 avg (+1.3), compared to conventional FT at 73.4 (-1.2) — a 2.5 point swing.

Per-Domain Breakdown

Table 2: Detailed mathematical and code performance tuning Qwen-3-8B and Llama-3.1-8B on domain-specific datasets.
What This Shows

This table breaks down performance on individual Math-7 and Code-3 benchmarks when fine-tuning with domain-specific datasets (Code-Z1, LIMO, OpenR1-Math). Green arrows indicate gains and red arrows indicate drops relative to the vanilla INSTRUCT baseline.

Key finding: Shadow-FT (labeled "Shadow") consistently achieves more green arrows and fewer red arrows than conventional LoRA across both model families and all three training datasets.

Key Takeaway

Shadow-FT improvements are broadly distributed across individual benchmarks — it is not winning on just one metric but improving across AIME24, GSM8K, MATH, HumanEval, LiveCodeBench and more.

LoRA vs Full-Parameter in Shadow-FT

An interesting pattern emerges: Shadow-FT LoRA sometimes outperforms Shadow-FT full-parameter, particularly on math benchmarks. This suggests that LoRA's low-rank constraint acts as a beneficial regularizer, preventing the model from overfitting to training data patterns that do not generalize to math reasoning.

Extended Model Zoo

Table 6: Extended results across additional model families: Falcon3 (3B, 10B), Gemma-3 (4B, 12B), Yi (6B), and Yi-Coder (9B).
What This Shows

This table extends the evaluation to Falcon, Gemma, and Yi model families. Shadow-FT achieves the best average score (bold) in most model configurations, demonstrating that the method generalizes beyond the Qwen and Llama families tested in the main experiments.

Notable results include Gemma-3-4B where conventional FT drops to 44.64 avg but Shadow-FT achieves 52.55 (+7.91 swing), and Yi-Coder-9B where Shadow-FT achieves 44.07 vs conventional FT at 39.26 (+4.81 swing).

Evaluation Benchmarks

Table 5: Details of the 19 evaluation benchmarks grouped by Math-7, Code-3, and Knowledge-9 categories.
Infrastructure Details

All experiments were conducted on 8x NVIDIA A100 80GB GPUs. Training used LLaMA-Factory framework. Evaluation used OpenCompass with standardized settings. Training data: UltraChat-200k (SFT) and UltraFeedback (DPO).

§ 6

Analysis Deep-Dives

Key Insight
Shadow-FT scales positively with LoRA rank (while conventional FT degrades), works on domain-specific data (medical, code, math), extends to DPO alignment, and applies to multimodal models.

1. LoRA Rank Scaling

One of the most telling analyses: as LoRA rank increases, Shadow-FT consistently improves while conventional fine-tuning on INSTRUCT degrades. Higher rank means more parameters updated, which for INSTRUCT leads to more aggressive disruption of its instruction-following prior, but for BASE allows richer learning.

Figure 3: Performance vs LoRA rank for Llama-3.2-1B — Shadow-FT improves with rank while conventional FT degrades.
What This Shows

The x-axis shows LoRA rank (4, 8, 16, 32, 64, 128, 256, 512), and the y-axis shows average benchmark score. Shadow-FT (blue solid line) trends upward from 30.26 at rank 4 to 32.03 at rank 512. Conventional FT (orange dashed line) trends downward from 30.11 at rank 4 to 28.47 at rank 512.

How to Read This
Start here: Note the divergence between the two lines. At rank 4 they are close (30.26 vs 30.11), but by rank 512 the gap is 3.56 points (32.03 vs 28.47).
Key observation: The vanilla INSTRUCT baseline (dotted line at 29.97) is crossed by conventional FT around rank 16, meaning higher ranks actively harm the model. Shadow-FT stays above it at all ranks.
Key Takeaway

Shadow-FT removes a fundamental bottleneck: practitioners no longer need to carefully limit LoRA rank to avoid INSTRUCT degeneration. Higher rank = better results with Shadow-FT.

2. Domain-Specific Adaptation

Shadow-FT is not limited to general-purpose SFT data. It also works on domain-specific datasets including medical, code, and mathematical domains.

Figure 4: Medical domain performance when tuning Llama-3.2-1B on Medical-o1-reasoning-SFT dataset.
What This Shows

This radar chart plots performance across six medical benchmarks (MedMCQA, MedQA, PubMedQA, MMLU Biology, GPQA-Medical, MMLU Pro Medical). Shadow-FT (blue outer polygon) has the largest area, outperforming both vanilla INSTRUCT (pink) and conventional LoRA (gray) across most medical sub-tasks.

Key Takeaway

Shadow-FT's benefits extend beyond general-purpose tuning to specialized domains like medicine, making it applicable for real-world domain adaptation scenarios.

Core Idea

Shadow-FT's advantage grows in two dimensions: with model capacity (higher LoRA rank) and with domain specialization (specific training data). This suggests the method is most valuable exactly where practitioners need it most — when pushing models to learn new, specialized capabilities.

3. Shadow-DPO: Extension to Alignment

Shadow-FT extends naturally to Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). Instead of running DPO on the INSTRUCT model, run it on BASE and transplant the delta. The authors call this Shadow-DPO.

Table 3: Shadow-DPO results for Llama-3.1-8B with LoRA ranks 8 and 128.
What This Shows

This table compares conventional DPO (applied to INSTRUCT) with Shadow-DPO (DPO on BASE, delta transplanted to INSTRUCT). At rank 8, Shadow-DPO achieves 54.96 vs DPO's 54.80. At rank 128, Shadow-DPO achieves 55.39 vs DPO's 54.62 — both above the vanilla baseline of 54.77.

Key Takeaway

The Shadow framework is not limited to SFT — it generalizes to preference optimization methods like DPO, and the advantage grows with higher LoRA rank.

4. Multimodal Extension

Shadow-FT also extends to multimodal large language models (MLLMs), where fine-tuning the base vision-language model and transplanting deltas to the instruct variant yields similar benefits.

Table 4: Multimodal extension — Shadow-FT applied to Gemma-3 and Llama-3.2-Vision on ChartQA.
What This Shows

Shadow-FT achieves the best scores across all four model configurations: Gemma-3 12B (54.92), Gemma-3 27B (63.80), Llama-3.2-Vision 11B (74.12), and Llama-3.2-Vision 90B (80.60). The improvement over conventional LoRA is especially clear in the larger models.

Key Takeaway

Shadow-FT is modality-agnostic — the principle of "train on BASE, apply to INSTRUCT" works for both text-only and multimodal models.

Scaling Factor Exploration

The authors explored using a scaling factor \(\lambda\) to control the magnitude of the transplanted delta: \(W_I^+ = W_I + \lambda \cdot \Delta W\). They found that \(\lambda = 1.0\) (no scaling) is optimal or near-optimal across all tested configurations. This simplifies practical usage: no hyperparameter search needed for the transplantation step.

Technical Detail

The robustness of \(\lambda = 1.0\) further confirms the weight similarity hypothesis. Since BASE and INSTRUCT occupy nearly the same point in weight space, a delta calibrated from one transfers at full magnitude to the other without overshooting or undershooting.

Detailed Numbers: Shadow-DPO ▼

Shadow-DPO was tested on Llama-3.1-8B using UltraFeedback as the preference dataset. Key observations:

  • At rank 8: Shadow-DPO (54.96) vs DPO (54.80) vs Vanilla (54.77) — modest but consistent gain.
  • At rank 128: Shadow-DPO (55.39) vs DPO (54.62) vs Vanilla (54.77) — conventional DPO actually degrades below vanilla at high rank, while Shadow-DPO continues to improve.
  • This mirrors the LoRA rank scaling pattern from SFT: higher rank helps Shadow methods but hurts conventional methods.
Detailed Numbers: Multimodal Results ▼

The multimodal experiments tested Gemma-3 and Llama-3.2-Vision on ChartQA with LoRA rank 128:

  • Gemma-3 12B: Vanilla 37.36, LoRA 53.48, Shadow-FT 54.92 (+1.44 over LoRA)
  • Gemma-3 27B: Vanilla 41.92, LoRA 60.28, Shadow-FT 63.80 (+3.52 over LoRA)
  • Llama-3.2-Vision 11B: Vanilla 22.12, LoRA 74.44, Shadow-FT 74.12 (comparable)
  • Llama-3.2-Vision 90B: Vanilla 30.92, LoRA 79.92, Shadow-FT 80.60 (+0.68 over LoRA)

The Gemma-3 results show the largest improvements, suggesting that models with less extensive post-training may benefit more from Shadow-FT in the multimodal setting.

§ 7

Insights & Takeaways

Core Insights
  1. Post-training changes weights minimally but optimization behavior dramatically.
  2. BASE models are better learners; INSTRUCT models are better executors.
  3. Weight updates are transferable between nearly-identical model variants.

Practical Recommendations

When to Use Shadow-FT
  • You are fine-tuning an INSTRUCT model and seeing marginal gains or degradation
  • Domain adaptation (medical, legal, code, math) where you want specialized knowledge without losing instruction-following
  • A BASE variant of your target INSTRUCT model is publicly available
  • You want to use higher LoRA ranks without performance degradation
  • You need a drop-in replacement for conventional fine-tuning (literally zero extra cost)
When NOT to Use Shadow-FT
  • No BASE model available: Some model providers only release INSTRUCT variants. Shadow-FT requires the BASE model for training.
  • Very small models (<1B): The weight similarity assumption may not hold as strongly for very small models where post-training represents a larger fraction of total capacity.
  • Radically different architectures: If the BASE and INSTRUCT use different tokenizers or architectures (rare but possible), weight delta transfer is not meaningful.
  • You need to significantly change behavior: If the goal is to fundamentally alter the model's personality or capabilities (not just improve them), the instruction prior you are bypassing might actually be helpful.

Open Questions

  • Does Shadow-FT work with continued pre-training? The paper tests SFT and DPO, but continued pre-training on large domain corpora is untested.
  • What about models with very different post-training? Models with heavy RLHF or constitutional AI training might have larger sigma values, potentially reducing Shadow-FT's effectiveness.
  • Compositional deltas: Can you combine Shadow-FT deltas from multiple domains? The paper hints at this but does not fully explore it.
  • Theoretical grounding: Why exactly does the instruction prior create gradient collapse? A formal analysis of the loss landscape geometry would strengthen the story.
§ 8

Related Work

Shadow-FT sits within a broader family of methods that manipulate weight spaces across model variants. Understanding these connections clarifies what is novel about Shadow-FT.

Comparison with Related Methods

Method Core Idea Difference from Shadow-FT
Chat Vector Extract the "chat delta" (INSTRUCT - BASE) and apply to other models for cross-lingual transfer Chat Vector transplants post-training deltas; Shadow-FT transplants fine-tuning deltas. Different direction of transfer.
Task Vectors Arithmetic operations on task-specific weight deltas for composing capabilities Task Vectors compose deltas from multiple tasks; Shadow-FT computes a single delta from a different starting point (BASE vs INSTRUCT).
Proxy-Tuning Use a small tuned model to steer a large base model at inference time via logit arithmetic Proxy-Tuning modifies outputs at inference time (added compute per token). Shadow-FT modifies weights once (zero added inference cost).
RE-Adapt Re-adapt LoRA modules trained on one model to another via alignment RE-Adapt requires explicit adapter alignment steps. Shadow-FT needs no adaptation — direct loading works because of weight similarity.
What Makes Shadow-FT Unique

Shadow-FT is distinguished by its simplicity and zero-cost nature. Unlike Proxy-Tuning (added inference cost), Task Vectors (requires multiple task-specific models), or RE-Adapt (requires alignment procedures), Shadow-FT requires only choosing which model to fine-tune. The insight that BASE models are better optimization substrates, combined with the observation that weight deltas transfer directly, makes this the simplest possible intervention.

Broader Context

Shadow-FT contributes to our understanding of the relationship between pre-training, post-training, and fine-tuning. The finding that post-training creates optimization barriers despite minimal weight changes has implications for:

  • Model merging: If instruction priors create optimization rigidity, model merging techniques should account for this when combining INSTRUCT models.
  • Post-training design: Future post-training pipelines might be designed to explicitly preserve optimization amenability while adding instruction-following capabilities.
  • Continual learning: The gradient collapse pattern in INSTRUCT models resembles catastrophic forgetting barriers, suggesting connections to continual learning research.
Shadow-FT Guide
Based on Taiqiang Wu, Runming Yang, Jiayi Li, Pengfei Hu, Yik-Chung Wu, Ngai Wong, Yujiu Yang (2025) • 2505.12716
Generated by Paper Dapper
